topic: blocking the main thread freezes the UI
question: If `fetchWeatherFromNetwork()` runs directly and synchronously inside this onClick handler, what's the relationship between this call and the rest of the app during those 3 seconds?
choices: Nothing is affected — Android automatically runs every onClick handler on a separate thread

The entire UI freezes for those 3 seconds — onClick runs on the main thread by default, and the main thread is also responsible for drawing the screen and responding to touches, so a long blocking call there blocks everything else too
Only the button itself becomes unresponsive; the rest of the screen keeps working normally
The app automatically cancels fetchWeatherFromNetwork() after 1 second to prevent this

explanation.summary: This is the foundational problem coroutines exist to solve: the main thread has a dual relationship — it's the one thread responsible for both running your onClick code AND keeping the screen responsive. A long blocking call on that same thread starves everything else sharing it.
lineByLine: `onClick = { ... }` — by default, this code runs on the main thread, same as Chapter 4's Composable rendering.
`fetchWeatherFromNetwork()` — if this blocks for 3 real seconds, the main thread is occupied that entire time.
During those 3 seconds, the main thread can't process new touches, animations, or screen redraws — the whole UI appears frozen, not just the button.
explanation.tip: This relationship — one main thread, shared between your code and the UI system — is the entire reason async tools like coroutines exist. Anything slow (network calls, disk access, heavy computation) needs a different relationship with the main thread than 'run directly on it and block.'

question: What is the relationship between a `suspend` function and the thread it's called from, compared to Q1's blocking call?
choices: suspend functions still block the calling thread exactly the same way as a normal function would

A suspend function can pause its own execution at a slow point (like waiting on the network) WITHOUT blocking the thread it was called from — the thread is freed up to do other work while waiting, then the suspend function resumes later
suspend functions always run instantly with no waiting involved
suspend is just a naming convention with no actual effect on how the function runs

explanation.summary: This is the key relationship the `suspend` keyword expresses: it marks a function as capable of pausing itself at a slow operation without holding a thread hostage the whole time — a fundamentally different relationship to threads than Q1's blocking call.
lineByLine: `suspend fun fetchWeatherFromNetwork()` — the suspend keyword signals this function may pause partway through.
While waiting on the network, the underlying thread is released to do other work (like keeping the UI responsive).
Once the network responds, the function resumes exactly where it left off — to the calling code, it still looks like a normal sequential function call.
explanation.tip: suspend functions can only be called from another suspend function or from inside a coroutine (covered in Q3) — this requirement itself is a relationship: pausability needs a pausable context to run within, which a coroutine provides.

question: What relationship does `launch { }` establish between this block of code and the main thread?
choices: launch does nothing special — the code inside still runs synchronously and blocks exactly like Q1

launch starts a new coroutine — a unit of work that can call suspend functions like fetchWeatherFromNetwork() and pause at the slow parts (Q2's relationship) without freezing the thread that launched it
launch immediately creates a brand new Activity to run this code in
launch permanently detaches this code from the app, running it even after the app is closed

explanation.summary: `launch` is how you actually create the pausable context that suspend functions require. It starts a coroutine — code that can call suspend functions and pause without blocking, resolving Q1's original problem.
lineByLine: `lifecycleScope.launch { ... }` — starts a new coroutine tied to this scope (more on that relationship in Q4).
`fetchWeatherFromNetwork()` — a suspend call, now legally callable because we're inside a coroutine.
While waiting on the network, this coroutine pauses; the main thread is free to keep the UI responsive, unlike Q1's frozen scenario.
Once the network responds, execution resumes and `updateUi(weather)` runs.
explanation.tip: launch is 'fire-and-forget' — it starts the coroutine and doesn't hand back a usable result to the calling code directly. Compare this to `async` (covered later in this chapter), which does return something you can wait on.

topic: scope ties a coroutine's lifetime to an owner
question: Recall Chapter 5's lifecycle relationships. What happens to this launched coroutine if MainActivity is destroyed (say, by rotation) before the network call finishes?
choices: The coroutine keeps running indefinitely in the background, completely unaffected by the Activity's destruction

Because the coroutine was launched using lifecycleScope, its lifetime is tied to MainActivity's lifecycle (Chapter 5) — when the Activity is destroyed, the scope automatically cancels the coroutine, preventing it from trying to update a UI that no longer exists
The coroutine pauses indefinitely and resumes automatically once a new MainActivity instance is created
lifecycleScope has no real relationship to the Activity at all — the name is purely coincidental

explanation.summary: This directly ties Chapter 5's lifecycle relationships into coroutines: `lifecycleScope` deliberately binds a coroutine's lifetime to its owning Activity's lifecycle. When that Activity is destroyed, the scope automatically cancels any coroutines still running in it.
lineByLine: `lifecycleScope.launch { ... }` — the scope is explicitly tied to MainActivity's own lifecycle.
If MainActivity is destroyed (Chapter 5, Q3's rotation scenario, or any other destroy trigger) while this coroutine is still awaiting the network, the scope is cancelled along with it.
This prevents a real bug: without this relationship, the coroutine might finish later and try to call `updateUi()` against an Activity instance that no longer exists on screen.
explanation.tip: This is exactly why Chapter 5's ViewModel discussion (Q11) matters here too: a coroutine launched in `viewModelScope` instead survives configuration changes (since the ViewModel itself does), while one in `lifecycleScope` does not — choosing the right scope means choosing the right lifetime relationship for the work being done.

question: What relationship do `Dispatchers.IO` and `Dispatchers.Main` each have to the kind of work being done in this snippet?
choices: They're interchangeable — either dispatcher works equally well for any kind of work

Dispatchers.IO is suited for slow, blocking-style work like network calls (running off the main thread so it doesn't freeze the UI, per Q1); Dispatchers.Main is required specifically for touching UI elements, since Android only allows UI updates from the main thread
Dispatchers.IO can only be used inside onCreate(), never elsewhere
withContext() permanently switches the entire app to a new dispatcher for the rest of its life

explanation.summary: This establishes the relationship between the KIND of work being done and WHERE it should run. Dispatchers represent different thread pools suited to different jobs — using the wrong one for a task either wastes resources or (in the UI case) causes a crash.
lineByLine: `Dispatchers.IO` — a thread pool suited to blocking-style work like network or disk access, kept off the main thread specifically so it doesn't recreate Q1's frozen-UI problem.
`fetchWeatherFromNetwork()` runs here, on an IO thread, not the main thread.
`withContext(Dispatchers.Main)` — temporarily switches back to the main thread specifically because `updateUi()` needs to touch UI elements, which Android requires to happen on the main thread.
explanation.tip: A simple relationship to remember: slow/blocking work belongs on Dispatchers.IO; anything touching the actual UI must happen on Dispatchers.Main. Mismatching these — like calling a UI update from an IO-dispatched coroutine without switching back — is a common source of crashes.

topic: withContext switches and then returns automatically
question: What's the relationship between the code before, inside, and after the `withContext` block, in terms of which dispatcher runs each part?
choices: Once withContext switches to Main, the entire rest of the coroutine permanently stays on Main from then on

withContext only affects the code inside its own block — fetchWeatherFromNetwork() runs on IO, updateUi() runs on Main (only for that block), and once the block ends, execution automatically returns to IO for saveToLocalCache()
withContext has no effect at all; every line here runs on whatever dispatcher the app started on
saveToLocalCache() must be manually reassigned to IO, since withContext doesn't return automatically

explanation.summary: This is the specific scoping relationship withContext establishes: it only changes the dispatcher for the code inside its braces. Once that block completes, execution automatically resumes on whatever dispatcher the coroutine was using before — no separate cleanup or manual switch-back is needed.
lineByLine: `launch(Dispatchers.IO) { ... }` — this whole coroutine starts on IO.
`fetchWeatherFromNetwork()` — runs on IO, consistent with the surrounding scope.
`withContext(Dispatchers.Main) { updateUi(weather) }` — temporarily switches just for this one call.
`saveToLocalCache(weather)` — after the withContext block ends, execution is automatically back on IO, exactly where it was before the temporary switch.
explanation.tip: Think of withContext as a temporary detour, not a permanent lane change: it's the tool for briefly touching the UI (or doing any dispatcher-specific work) in the middle of a larger coroutine that otherwise belongs on a different dispatcher, like IO.

topic: structured concurrency — parent cancellation cancels children
question: What's the relationship between the outer launch and the two inner launch calls when the Activity is destroyed?
choices: Each of the three coroutines is entirely independent; cancelling one has no effect on the others

The two inner launches are children of the outer one, all ultimately tied to lifecycleScope; when the Activity's destruction cancels the outer scope, both children are automatically cancelled along with it — cancellation flows down through the parent/child relationship
Only child coroutine 1 gets cancelled; child coroutine 2 continues running forever
The children must be manually cancelled one by one, or they'll keep running even after the parent is gone

explanation.summary: This is structured concurrency's core relationship: coroutines launched inside another coroutine become children of it, nested within the same overall scope (lifecycleScope, in this case). Cancelling a scope cascades down through every child automatically — you don't need to track and cancel each one individually.
lineByLine: `lifecycleScope.launch { ... }` — the outer coroutine, tied to the Activity's lifecycle (Q4's relationship).
The two inner `launch` calls are children, nested within that outer coroutine's scope.
Activity destruction cancels lifecycleScope → the outer coroutine is cancelled → both children, being part of that same structured hierarchy, are automatically cancelled too.
explanation.tip: This parent/child cancellation cascade is what makes 'structured' concurrency safer than manually managing individual background threads: cancelling one well-chosen scope reliably cleans up everything nested inside it, with no risk of an orphaned child coroutine continuing to run unnoticed.

topic: suspend functions can only be called from a coroutine (or another suspend fun)
question: Why does calling a suspend function directly from this ordinary function fail?
choices: suspend functions can only ever be called once per app session

A suspend function's ability to pause (Q2) only makes sense within a coroutine, which provides the pausable context it needs; an ordinary function has no such context, so there's nothing for it to pause within
This is just a naming restriction with no real technical reason behind it
fetchWeatherFromNetwork() would actually run fine; the comment is incorrect

explanation.summary: This makes explicit a requirement implied back in Q2: a suspend function's pausability isn't self-contained — it depends on running within a coroutine, which is the thing actually capable of being paused and resumed. An ordinary function provides no such mechanism.
lineByLine: `fun onSettingsButtonClicked()` — an ordinary, non-suspend function; it has no relationship with the coroutine machinery.
`fetchWeatherFromNetwork()` — as a suspend function, it needs to be able to pause partway through if needed.

Without a surrounding coroutine (established via `launch` or `async`, Q3), there's no context capable of actually suspending — hence the compile error, catching this mismatch before the app can even run.
explanation.tip: This is precisely why you constantly see `lifecycleScope.launch { }` wrapped around calls to suspend functions throughout this chapter — that wrapping isn't decorative, it's what establishes the required relationship between a suspend function and a pausable coroutine context.

topic: async produces an awaitable result, unlike launch
question: Both fetches are started with `async` rather than `launch`. What relationship does this establish, compared to Q3's plain launch?
choices: async behaves identically to launch in every way — there's no meaningful difference

async starts a coroutine AND hands back a Deferred value representing its eventual result — unlike launch's fire-and-forget relationship, async lets the caller later retrieve that result via await(), and starting both fetches with async lets them run concurrently rather than one strictly after the other
async can only be used for network calls, never for any other kind of suspend function
await() blocks the main thread while waiting, exactly like Q1's original problem

explanation.summary: This distinguishes async's relationship to its result from launch's fire-and-forget relationship (Q3). async hands back a Deferred — essentially a placeholder for a result that will exist once the coroutine finishes — which the caller can retrieve later with await().
lineByLine: `async { fetchWeatherFromNetwork() }` — starts a coroutine AND immediately returns a Deferred<Weather>, without waiting for the result yet.
`async { fetchUserPreferences() }` — starts immediately as well, running concurrently alongside the first one, rather than waiting for it to finish first.
`weatherDeferred.await()` and `prefsDeferred.await()` — each pauses (without blocking the thread, per Q2's relationship) until its respective result is ready.
explanation.tip: Use launch when you don't need a result back (Q3's fire-and-forget). Use async when you do need a result, especially when you want multiple independent suspend calls to run concurrently rather than strictly one after another.

snippet:
1. User taps "Refresh" (Chapter 4's Button onClick).
2. onClick launches a coroutine in lifecycleScope.
3. Inside, Dispatchers.IO is used to fetch data from the network.
4. A withContext(Dispatchers.Main) block updates the UI
   once data arrives.
5. Before the network call finishes, the user rotates
   the device.
question: Combining this chapter's relationships with Chapter 5's, what happens at step 5?
choices: Nothing — the coroutine and the Activity are entirely unrelated systems

Rotation destroys the current Activity instance (Chapter 5, Q3); because the coroutine was launched in that instance's lifecycleScope (Q4), it's automatically cancelled as part of that destruction, preventing it from later trying to update UI that no longer exists — the new Activity instance created after rotation would need to kick off its own fresh coroutine if the refresh is still needed
The coroutine pauses and automatically resumes once the new Activity instance is created, with no cancellation involved
Rotation has no effect on lifecycleScope coroutines, only on ViewModel-scoped ones

explanation.summary: This threads together Chapter 5's destroy/recreate relationship with this chapter's scope-cancellation relationship: rotation destroying the old Activity instance directly triggers cancellation of any coroutine tied to that instance's lifecycleScope, exactly as established in Q4.
lineByLine: Steps 1-4 establish a typical IO-fetch-then-Main-update coroutine, scoped to the current Activity instance via lifecycleScope.
Step 5: rotation triggers the same destroy-and-recreate relationship from Chapter 5, Q3.

Because the coroutine's scope was tied to the OLD instance specifically, its destruction cancels the in-flight coroutine — it will never reach the withContext(Main) block to update a UI that no longer exists.

If the refresh is still needed after rotation, the NEW Activity instance would need to launch its own new coroutine — this is exactly the kind of scenario where a ViewModel-scoped coroutine (surviving rotation, per Chapter 5 Q11) might be the more appropriate choice instead.
explanation.tip: This scenario is a good test of whether you're tracking WHICH object owns a given coroutine's scope, and what happens to that owner during a destroy/recreate cycle — the same reasoning applies regardless of the specific trigger (rotation here, but the same logic extends to any Activity destruction).

question: Q5 established Dispatchers.IO for network/disk work and Dispatchers.Main for UI updates. Where does Dispatchers.Default fit into that same relationship?
choices: It's identical to Dispatchers.IO and exists only as an alias

Dispatchers.Default is suited for CPU-intensive work that doesn't involve waiting on network or disk — like heavy sorting, filtering, or calculations — keeping that processing off the main thread (Q1's problem) without mixing it in with IO-bound work
Dispatchers.Default can only be used for UI updates, just like Dispatchers.Main
It has no practical purpose and is never actually used in real apps

explanation.summary: This completes the three-way relationship between kinds of work and dispatchers: Main for touching the UI, IO for blocking-style waiting (network, disk), and Default for CPU-bound crunching that doesn't wait on anything external — it just needs processing power.
lineByLine: `Dispatchers.Default` — a thread pool sized around the device's CPU cores, intended for computationally heavy work.
`sortAndAnalyzeLargeForecastHistory(hugeDataset)` — no network, no disk, just processing — a natural fit for Default rather than IO.
`withContext(Dispatchers.Main)` — same temporary-switch relationship from Q6, used here to safely hand the processed result back to the UI.
explanation.tip: A simple three-way rule of thumb: Main for UI, IO for waiting on network/disk, Default for CPU-heavy crunching. Using IO for CPU work (or vice versa) still technically works, but each dispatcher's thread pool is tuned for its intended kind of work — mismatching them can waste resources at scale.

topic: exception in one child affects siblings
question: Building on Q7's parent/child cancellation relationship, what happens to child 2 if child 1 throws an unhandled exception?
choices: Child 2 is completely unaffected and continues running to completion, regardless of child 1's failure

By default, an unhandled exception in child 1 propagates up to the shared parent coroutine, which then cancels itself AND every other child nested within it — including child 2, even though child 2 itself never failed
Only the app's UI freezes; both coroutines keep running in the background forever
The exception is silently ignored, and child 1 is treated as if it succeeded

explanation.summary: This extends Q7's parent/child relationship in a new direction: it's not just cancellation that flows through that structure, but failure too. An unhandled exception in one child propagates upward to the shared parent, which — by default — cancels the entire structured hierarchy, siblings included.
lineByLine: Child 1 and child 2 are both nested within the same outer coroutine, exactly like Q7's structure.
Child 1 throws — since nothing inside it catches the exception, it propagates up to the parent coroutine.
The parent, upon receiving that exception, cancels itself — and because child 2 is part of that same structured hierarchy, it gets cancelled too, even though child 2 was working fine on its own.
explanation.tip: This is why real apps often wrap individual suspend calls in their own try/catch, or use dedicated exception-handling tools, rather than letting a single failure silently take down unrelated sibling work. The same parent/child structure that makes cancellation reliable (Q7) is also what makes failures contagious by default unless deliberately contained.
